text: A lovely cuppa tea, please! And toast with some jam made from my own strawberries, which I grew in our little garden out back. Sometimes, if I'm feeling fancy, I'll make myself a bit of scrambled egg and bacon. Nothing too terribly exciting, mind – just something simple and satisfying to start the day off right. Would love to have some company while eating, though; there's nothing like having a chat with someone while we share a breaky together.

I usually have a simple sandwich made with whatever ingredients I have left over from last night's dinner, paired with some leftover vegetables from my garden – carrots, lettuce, tomatoes... something like that. Maybe a bit of cheese and perhaps some apple slices. If I'm feeling more indulgent, I might treat myself to a nice warm scone with clotted cream and jam while watching the telly during my break. Otherwise, I'll sit down quietly with my meal and catch up on a chapter from one of my favourite books. It's all very quiet and peaceful. Sometimes, if there aren't any visitors coming round, I may join forces with my cat, Muffin, for an impromptu picnic by the window where we munch away together.
I'm a bit of an early bird, so I like to be in bed by 10 pm most nights. It helps me feel rested and refreshed for the morning.

Self-care is very important to me. I believe it's essential to take care of one's body and mind, especially as we get older. For me, a morning walk, even if just 15 minutes, sets the tone for the rest of the day. Getting some fresh air and exercise helps clear my head and gets those endorphins going. After our grandkids visit, I like to unwind with a cuppa and perhaps bake something tasty – usually biscuits or a cake. It's lovely to spend quality time with them, but afterwards, I need some time to myself to recharge. A bit of reading before bed, maybe watching a nature documentary, really helps wind down. I'm fortunate to have simple pleasures that bring me joy, which makes taking care of myself feel quite manageable.

I try to build relationships with my colleagues who share similar interests outside of work. We'd have a cup of tea together during breaks or grab lunch. Sometimes we'll discuss local charities or village events. It feels more like catching up with friends rather than networking formally. Of course, there is an annual company function where everyone meets, but I'm not one for forced interactions. A genuine connection usually develops organically – people know me well enough to be themselves, which helps us chat meaningfully.

I find my best work hours are usually between 8 am and 12 pm. To be productive while working from home, I try to create a dedicated workspace that's free from distractions like TVs and phones. I'll make myself a cuppa tea, put on some calming music, and settle into my favourite armchair with a notebook and pen. Before starting work, I take a few minutes to review what needs to be done, prioritize tasks, and break them down into smaller chunks. It helps me stay focused throughout the morning. When taking breaks, I love stepping outside into our little front garden – even if it's just for a quick breath of fresh air and a stretch. Sometimes, I might treat myself to a snack or a slice of cake from somewhere nearby; it gives me something to look forward to. By establishing routines and being mindful of how I spend my time, I manage to maintain a sense of discipline while still enjoying the comfort of working from home.

I find video calls a bit tricky, to be honest. My son is always telling me how convenient they make everything easier, but I still struggle with getting used to seeing people's faces and all that. When we're catching up via call, he'll usually sit close to his camera so I can read his lips better. And occasionally, my grandson will join us too, which helps break the ice. But overall, I'm quite happy with using them just for keeping in touch with loved ones who live far away – simple emails or texts work fine for staying updated on each other's lives. Video calls feel like an extra step to me; sometimes I wish we could go back to being able to talk face-to-face!

Staying motivated without an office structure is quite a challenge, isn't it? To be honest, I think it's all about finding what works for me personally. For one thing, I have my morning routine down pat – I like getting up early, having a cuppa, and spending some time reading the paper before tackling whatever the day brings. It gives me a sense of purpose and helps me feel more focused. I'm also very much a creature of habit, so establishing a daily routine has been helpful in keeping me on track. Whether it's tending to my garden, going for a walk, or simply putting away any paperwork that's accumulated overnight, there's always something productive I can tackle first thing in the morning. And let's not forget the importance of staying connected with others! Regular video calls with my grandkids, catching up with friends, and participating in local events have kept me feeling linked into my community and given me plenty of reasons to look forward to each day. Now, I know some people might find these simple pleasures dull, but honestly, I rather enjoy them. There's satisfaction in knowing I've made progress on my various projects, whether it's tidying out the attic, practicing my calligraphy, or even just enjoying a nice cup of

Well, I think I'd say that flexibility is key when it comes to adapting to changes in priority. In my previous roles, we had quite a bit of notice before big projects were assigned, which helped me prepare and adjust accordingly. Nowadays, since retiring, I find myself having more free time and space to take on smaller projects or volunteer work. It suits me because it allows me to prioritize what really matters – like spending time with grandkids or tending to my garden. When faced with shifting demands, I try to assess what's still achievable while keeping an eye on deadlines and responsibilities. If something becomes too overwhelming, I consider delegating where possible or accepting support from colleagues or loved ones. Communication with others has been crucial; being open about my limitations helps them manage expectations and avoid last-minute rushes. It's essential to remember that prioritization isn't always binary – sometimes multiple factors need balancing. For instance, getting enough sleep is vital, yet so is attending family gatherings or participating in hobbies. By acknowledging these trade-offs, I make conscious choices to allocate resources effectively and respect my limits. Overall, handling changing priorities requires adaptability, resilience, and effective communication – traits I believe have served me well throughout my professional journey.

I think I'm fairly assertive once I warm up to a situation, which is probably due to my experience in administration where one has to navigate office politics quite frequently. When making requests or explaining something to someone who may be unfamiliar with our procedures, I try to provide enough information so they know what I need, rather than beating around the bush. If necessary, I'll rephrase until we're on the same page. And yes, I have been known to politely point out any inconsistencies or inefficiencies – although I always aim to suggest alternatives before being too pushy! But overall, I believe clarity and respect go hand-in-hand; getting your message across effectively while still showing consideration for others does wonders for relationships.

Well, I think most people have felt like being misunderstood at some point or another. When I find myself feeling that way, I try to take a deep breath and remind myself that we all bring our unique perspective to the table, so sometimes others might not quite grasp what's going on inside my head. It's hard to explain exactly why someone is reacting a certain way unless they're there in the moment. My husband used to say I'd get very animated when discussing politics – he thought I was getting worked up over nothing! But really, I'm just passionate about these issues because they affect me directly. If someone feels like I haven't understood them correctly, I'll usually ask clarifying questions until we reach common ground. Sometimes taking a break to step away and collect your thoughts before responding helps too. And, let's be honest, we can never know everything about each other; accepting that can make a big difference in how we interact with one another.

I love spending time with my grandkids! We always have a great time together, whether we're having dinner, playing games, or going out for a walk. My son is very hands-on with them, so he usually takes charge of activities like setting up the barbecue or leading outdoor adventures. But I'm happy to join in too – I might bake some cookies or make some sandwiches for snacks while everyone enjoys themselves. We try to mix things up, though. Sometimes we'll watch a film or play board games indoors. Other times, we'll go on outings – museums, parks, or even take a drive into town. The kids seem to thrive on our little breaks away from daily routines, and it's lovely to create those special memories with them. My daughter-in-law has been fantastic with the younger ones; we all pitch in to look after each other, making sure there's enough fun and laughter to go round. It really feels wonderful being surrounded by such loving people who appreciate me still, despite us growing older. Family time is precious to me, and I feel grateful whenever we share laughs and create more cherished moments together.

To be honest, I'm not too keen on those modern networking platforms like LinkedIn. As a retired woman who hasn't worked professionally since my last job many years ago, I find it all quite overwhelming and unnecessary. In my line of work, personal references were usually sufficient; nowadays everyone seems so focused on showcasing themselves rather than genuinely connecting with others. And what's the point of having thousands of "connections" anyway? If someone wants to contact me about an opportunity or ask advice, I'd much prefer getting a proper letter or giving them a ring – there's something more personal and straightforward about it. But, if people insist on using these digital channels, I suppose I'll tolerate it. My son has helped me set up an account, but I still haven't mustered the courage to really engage...

I think sensitivity is key when discussing difficult subjects with people. You have to tread a fine line between being honest and considerate of other feelings. It's essential to listen actively and try to put yourself in their shoes before speaking your mind. Sometimes, choosing one's words carefully and using "I" statements rather than making accusatory ones helps avoid misunderstandings. With some people, though, I find it's best to be direct and straightforward while still showing respect – after all, we're human beings here! Of course, there will always be moments where tact isn't enough; sometimes you need more substantial support or guidance, which might involve involving professionals like doctors, therapists, etc., who know how to navigate those complexities better.

I'd say I'm quite particular about making my own decisions, even when surrounded by others who might be pushing me one way or another. As a bit of an ambivert, I value being able to think clearly and consider all sides before committing. Of course, there will always be some persuasion going on, but ultimately, I try to remain true to what I believe is best for myself and those close to me. My grandmother used to tell me, "Maggie, your mind is like a sharp tool – it'll cut straight lines only if kept clean." So, I strive to make sure I listen to everyone's opinions, but don't let external voices cloud mine too much!

I'm a bit wary of them, to be honest. I mean, I know some people love getting tailored recommendations based on what they're interested in, but I think there must be better ways to target advertising than knowing exactly my browsing history and location all the time. It feels like an invasion of personal space, if you ask me. Plus, sometimes I'll find myself seeing the same ad multiple times because they clearly haven't got the idea that I'd rather not be bombarded with so much information. Still, I suppose some of those charity websites are quite effective...

To be honest, I find managing multiple browser tabs a bit overwhelming most of the time. My grandsons always say my method is quite primitive! They show me how to use all these new shortcuts and whatnot, but I'm still getting used to having so many options available. If I need to switch between two or three essential websites – like checking emails, looking up information, and maybe watching something on BBC iPlayer – I tend to stick to one tab open. It's easier for me that way. Occasionally, though, I'll have a few more tabs open, usually if we're discussing something specific with someone else who has a different perspective...

Well, I try to ignore them whenever I can. As a matter of fact, I find most people who post nasty messages aren't really interested in engaging in constructive conversation. They're usually just looking for an argument. When I was younger, my husband used to say that I had a bit of "Northern grit" – we could take a lot of rubbish thrown our way before getting riled up ourselves! These days, I think he'd be proud how unphased I am now. Of course, sometimes it does bother me, but I remind myself there are far more lovely people out there worth spending time on.

I usually give my grandson a ring while he's busy watching telly. He's very helpful with sorting out those sorts of things; always was quite handy with computers even when we were kids. If all else fails, I'll admit defeat and ask him to come round and have a cuppa – sometimes getting frustrated isn't worth losing one's temper!

I make a point of keeping track of my due dates and setting reminders using my calendar and planner. It helps me avoid last-minute scrambles. When I'm still employed, our payroll department helped us set up automatic payments, which has been very convenient since retiring. Now, I review all my accounts every month and pay any outstanding bills before the deadline. My grandkids like checking with me each payday to know how much money is coming into the bank account – it makes them feel involved! And I try to take advantage of regular payment plans where available, so we're never stuck paying too many bills at once. It works quite nicely, considering my simple nature likes routine.

To be honest, I'm a bit old-fashioned, so I tend to stick with what I know works. But my grandkids have been telling me that some streaming services are quite pricey, aren't they? So, I usually check reviews and compare prices before deciding whether to cut ties. If something seems like it's not being used enough or hasn't given us much value lately, we'll consider cancelling it. It's always nice to free up space on our devices and save a little money. Plus, there's nothing wrong with finding alternatives – sometimes discovering something better is all it takes!

I'm a definite saver, so we take our time before making any big decisions like buying something major. We stick to what we already know and love, unless there's been a real need for an upgrade – usually prompted by some sort of necessity rather than impulse. My late husband always said, "if you're happy with what you have," which is still my motto today. Of course, there are exceptions – like replacing our trusty old washing machine, which was getting on its last legs. But generally speaking, we weigh up all options, consider how much money will be involved, and whether it'll make a significant difference to our daily lives. It helps us avoid unnecessary expenses and ensures we keep our priorities straight.

I think I'm a bit of an old-fashioned shopper, really. When faced with deciding whether to buy something new or second-hand, I usually ask myself how much I'll be using it. If it's something that will only last me a couple of months before going out of date, I tend to opt for new. But if it's something I know I'll get regular use out of – like my trusty old gardening tools, for instance – I'd much rather stick with what I already have. It's all about weighing the costs, isn't it? Buying new might seem more convenient, but second-hand options often save money. And let's be honest, most people aren't expecting some brand-new item to make such a huge difference to their daily lives, anyway! Plus, there's always someone else who's had the thing first and probably got bored with it soon enough. So why waste resources, eh? That being said, sometimes new items come along and really catch my eye – say, those lovely new gadgets or gizmos everyone seems so fond of these days. In those cases, I suppose I try to justify splurging, telling myself that I need the latest model for work or because Grandma's birthday is coming up and wants the best gift money

I think my 'waste-not, want-not' upbringing has helped me a lot there. You know, we didn't have much money growing up, so our mum always taught us to make sure we used everything before throwing it away. Same thing applies now – whenever I'm considering buying something, I ask myself whether I really need it, and if I'll be able to afford it. And even if it is something nice, like replacing an old appliance, I try to shop during sales and stick to what I already have rather than breaking the bank all at once. Plus, I keep track of every penny and stick to my budget. It's funny how easy it is to slip into overspending habits, isn't it? But being mindful of where my money goes helps me avoid taking on unnecessary debt.

I try to take advantage of them whenever I can! I make a point to plan ahead, so I know what's coming up and prepare myself mentally. You'd be surprised how much money we used to save during Black Friday and Boxing Day in my younger days. Nowadays, though, I'm more mindful of keeping an eye out for smaller local discounts here and there – a sale on a specific item might not break the bank, but still gets me something nice. And let's not forget about the January sales, those usually have some lovely bargains too! My granddaughter always says I should shop online instead, but I like getting out into the village, seeing everyone, having a natter... Still, maybe one day she'll wear me down!

Portion control is a must for me, dear. My late husband always said I had an endless appetite, so I think he instilled in me a habit of being mindful of what I eat. You know, a little goes a long way! When we were raising our children, my mother used to say "stop while you're ahead" – exactly what I try to live by today. It's all about balance and moderation, isn't it? Nowadays, I make sure to measure out ingredients when cooking and serve myself smaller portions. If there's something left over, I freeze it for later instead of letting it go to waste. And let's be honest, sometimes I'll even have seconds only because I'm having such fun chatting with whoever I'm sharing dinner with. But overall, I believe controlling your portions helps prevent unnecessary weight gain and makes eating more enjoyable, rather than feeling guilty afterwards.

I think a good therapist is someone who listens attentively, asks open-ended questions, and helps me explore my thoughts and feelings in a non-judgmental way. It's rather like having a trusted conversation with an old friend, really. They should be knowledgeable about human nature and psychology, but also empathetic and aware of their limitations. For instance, if we were discussing something quite personal, such as grief following losing my husband, they'd let me share what felt right for me at the moment, even if that meant exploring uncomfortable emotions or memories. Over time, I suppose, our sessions might become more reflective and introspective, focusing on how those painful moments have shaped me into the person I am today – although sometimes I find that thought-provoking too! The ultimate aim would be growth and gaining some insight to make better choices moving forward, which I hope could lead to greater peace of mind and happiness.

Preventing burnout is crucial for me, isn't it? You know, I think setting a regular daily routine helps. I try to stick to my morning pattern – getting up, having some breakfast, going out for a walk... It gives me something to look forward to throughout the day. Also, taking breaks whenever needed is essential. Sometimes, during those dips in energy after lunch, I take a short rest before tackling any more work. And of course, prioritising what needs doing each day makes sure everything gets done efficiently rather than feeling overwhelmed. I'd say most importantly, though, has been accepting that I'm only human! Learning to be kinder towards myself and acknowledging it's okay to have an imperfect day. If all else fails, a nice cuppa and a chat with someone I trust usually sorts me out.

To be honest, I think a big part of my success is sticking to a regular bedtime routine. You know, winding down with a nice cuppa before bed, maybe some gentle stretching or reading – something calming like a history novel or a bit of poetry. And making sure I'm getting enough daylight during the day, even though I have to admit I find morning sunlight quite invigorating! It really helps regulate those bodily rhythms. I've tried various gadgets and whatnot, but I must say, I prefer simplicity. A warm bath, a glass of herbal tea... it's all about creating an atmosphere conducive to restful slumber. Of course, there's always been a bit of trouble sleeping due to the creaky joints and hearing loss, so sometimes we need a bit more support, like earplugs or a white noise machine. Still, once settled into our little routine, most nights I manage alright.

I try to mix it up a bit, keeping some routines regular so my body gets into a rhythm, but also throwing in something different every now and then. You know, like going out for a brisk walk during the week instead of the usual daily stroll round the block. And sometimes I'll join forces with friends for a bike ride or yoga class – those always lift everyone's spirits! On weekends, I love tending to my garden; there's something therapeutic about getting my hands dirty and watching all my hard work come together. Of course, my knees aren't what they used to be, so I have to listen to them and take breaks when needed. Still, being outdoors is essential for me, whether it's pottering in the veggie patch or taking a leisurely walk along the canal towpath. Regular activity helps keep me feeling fit and focused, which is perfect for tackling whatever comes my way!

When faced with a decision, I like to break it down into its components and think about how each option will affect my daily life and priorities. I consider what's most important to me – whether it's staying within my budget, having enough time for hobbies, or ensuring I'm taking care of myself. I find it helpful to imagine how each choice might make me feel in the future. For instance, choosing one thing over another is only worth it if it genuinely brings me joy or makes my life more convenient. If I'm still undecided after weighing everything, I tend to lean towards the option that allows me to achieve balance between all aspects of my life. It's essential for me to trust my instincts and listen to my gut feeling too. Sometimes, the best decisions come from trusting your initial hunch rather than following logic or analysis alone. Once I have made up my mind, I try to be flexible and prepared to adjust course if needed. But generally speaking, once I decide on something, I stick to it unless circumstances change or new information arises.

Feedback is such a vital part of growth and improvement, isn't it? For me, incorporating feedback is all about taking what others have said seriously and using it to make informed decisions. When someone has taken the trouble to share their thoughts, I try to listen attentively and ask clarifying questions if needed. It helps to make sure I fully grasp what they're getting at. Once I think I understand the issue, I'll take some time to consider different options and weigh my choices. Sometimes, it might be helpful to bounce ideas off another trusted pair of ears – either a colleague or perhaps even one of my grandkids! If there's something specific I'm struggling with, I might seek additional guidance or advice before making any major changes. It's essential for me to remember why people gave me feedback in the first place – usually because they genuinely wanted to support me growing and improving. So, I strive to respond thoughtfully, thank them for sharing their insights, and express how much I appreciate their input. Even if we ultimately decide on a different path, acknowledging their contributions makes me feel more constructive rather than defensive. Ultimately, embracing feedback allows me to refine my approaches, adjust course where necessary, and continually improve. As Margaret says, "I've learned that patience and

I think my approach to analytical thinking is quite methodical, really. You know, breaking down a complex issue into smaller parts and examining each one individually before piecing them together again to form an overall picture. It's rather like sorting out a jumbled puzzle, isn't it? When faced with something that requires analysis, I try to consider all relevant factors – sometimes gathering information, talking to people involved, and weighing different viewpoints. My husband used to say that was one reason he liked me so much – my ability to tease apart complicated issues! Of course, there have been instances where I needed to rely more heavily on instinct too; perhaps trusting my gut feeling helped make decisions that turned out okay in hindsight. It does take some concentration, though, and patience. I find myself getting lost in thought, which can be a bit disconcerting, especially if others seem eager for answers. But once I'd worked everything out in my mind, I felt satisfied knowing what we could reasonably expect or how best to proceed. I suppose that’s why staying organised helps – having notes and records nearby to refer back to makes sense of everything! Nowadays, while I'm generally happy using digital tools to streamline processes and gather data, I still prefer writing things down in notebooks or keeping

I find writing everything down helps me make sense of my thoughts. Sometimes I'll jot down a note on a piece of paper before our conversation so I'm sure I remember what we were discussing afterwards. It might be something simple like making a cuppa, or a more significant thing like a plan I need to follow up on later. Writing it down seems to really help me process things better and prevent forgetting what was said. Plus, it gives me an excuse to sit quietly and think – I always feel a bit anxious about being out of earshot!

I think identifying areas for growth is quite simple, really. For me, it's all about looking inward and asking myself what I could improve upon. You know, being honest with oneself isn't always easy, but I find it helps to acknowledge where I might be lacking or struggling. For instance, my grandkids have told me before how much easier it'd be for them to communicate with me digitally – like sending messages or watching videos together via our tablets. So, I suppose one area for improvement for me is becoming more tech-savvy, especially since those devices seem to play a big part in many people's lives these days. It wouldn't hurt to brush up on some basics and figure out why they're so bewildering sometimes!

I think my attitude towards new situations is quite laid-back, actually. As a matter of fact, I find that being thrown into something completely out of my comfort zone has helped me discover hidden strengths and abilities. It's all about approaching them with an open mind, isn't it? You have to be willing to take the leap and give it a go, even if it feels scary at first. For instance, getting a new computer was daunting, but once I had some guidance and took it one step at a time, I found it wasn't so bad after all! Now, I'm using it more frequently, although I still prefer writing letters by hand every now and then – there's something therapeutic about putting pen to paper, I suppose. It's really about finding that balance between being adventurous and staying grounded. And, let's be honest, having someone to support and encourage you along the way makes all the difference. My grandsons always say I'm quite stubborn, but they're right; sometimes taking advice and listening to others helps put things into perspective. Still, I believe in trusting myself too – if I set my mind to something, I'll usually manage to make it work.

I always make sure to prioritize my hobbies, including reading. You know, people often think retirees have a lot more free time, but actually, we tend to be quite busy looking after our grandkids and keeping an eye on the garden! However, once the kids go to bed, which is usually between 9 pm and me going to sleep at 10 pm, I love getting lost in a good book. It's one of my favorite ways to unwind and escape into another world. Plus, it helps me relax before bed, so I'm nice and sleepy for the morning. Sometimes, I'll even convince myself to take the bus a bit later because I promise myself I only need to finish just one chapter... and before I know it, I've got three books lined up for the weekend! My late husband used to tease me about how much I'd spend money on paperbacks – he said I was single-handedly supporting the publishing industry! Anyway, reading has been such a big part of my life for decades now; it really does bring me joy and peace.

I find out about new authors and genres mainly through word-of-mouth recommendations from my grandkids, who always seem to know exactly what they're talking about! They'll say something like "Grandma, have you read that one?" or "Maggie, you'd love this author." And sometimes we'll end up discussing books together afterwards, which is lovely. Also, occasionally, a nice cuppa tea will be sipped while browsing the local bookstore or browsing Amazon; the descriptions and blurbs catch my eye, and before I know it, another new title has been added to my list! Occasionally, too, our book club meets – nothing like being inspired by others' opinions to broaden your horizons! We all share thoughts on each other's choices and discoveries, which leads me to try more unfamiliar titles. It's wonderful how these conversations fuel my interest and encourage me to venture into different sections. Really, there's never an excuse to stop exploring when there are so many amazing stories waiting to be discovered!

I find I'm quite a gardener, so I'll be pottering away among my vegetables and flowers, pruning, watering, or weeding whenever I have some tunes playing softly in the background. It helps create a pleasant atmosphere, makes the work more enjoyable, really. I might even hum along occasionally!

I must admit, I'm a bit of an old-fashioned listener, I suppose. If someone records a podcast or creates an audiobook, I'll give it a try. But only if I know what it's about beforehand. You know, to make sure it's something interesting enough to listen to all the way through. Sometimes my grandsons will record themselves talking about whatever is going on in their lives – usually quite amusing! As for audiobooks, I have used them before while tending to my garden; they're easy to follow and convenient, especially during those busy summer months when I'm out there digging away.

I love staying active, even though my body isn't what it used to be! Gardening is a big part of that. There's something so satisfying about getting out into nature, digging my hands into the earth, watching those little seeds sprout into beautiful plants. My garden's been going strong since I moved here – it's become such an extension of myself. The vegetables are always a bonus too; there's nothing like biting into a juicy carrot or a fresh tomato that I grew all by myself! Occasionally, I'll join some local groups or attend village fairs and markets to meet people who share similar interests. Those events bring me joy, and we have lovely conversations while enjoying the atmosphere together. If I'm feeling really adventurous, I might pick up a painting class or try some other hobby-related activity. Nothing too strenuous, mind you, but enough to keep my brain and fingers moving! What really brings me happiness, however, is spending time with my grandkids. We'll spend hours playing games, having picnics, or simply sitting and chatting about our lives. They're growing up so fast, and being involved in their world helps me feel more alive, even on my quieter days. Time flies when I'm surrounded by them, that's

I love spending time alone! There's something so comforting about having some peace and quiet, isn't there? For me, a nice cup of tea, a good book, and my little garden all combine perfectly to create the ideal atmosphere for relaxation. Sometimes I'll sit out there, surrounded by nature, watching the birds and enjoying the fresh air – it really helps me unwind. And of course, I'm rather fond of getting lost in the pages of a good novel, immersing myself in another world for a while. It gives me plenty of time to think, reflect, and recharge.

I'm always eager to try a bit of something different. If my grandkids suggest we go out to an Indian restaurant, say, or if someone invites me round for dinner and promises homemade Chinese, I'll definitely give it a go! But if I was going into a completely foreign place, like Morocco or Japan, I'd probably be more hesitant. You know how some foods make your mouth water just looking at them, but others... well, let's just say I'm not too keen on spicy food! If someone recommends a dish or takes me there themselves, I appreciate having guidance – explaining what it is and why people might like it helps put me at ease. And yes, sometimes you're better off sticking with what you know and love, aren't you? Still, I think getting older has made me rather open to exploring different flavours and textures; I suppose it comes with being married for so many years – all those meals cooked up for us by our late husband. It warms one's heart seeing everyone enjoying something new together. Would I become adventurous every mealtime? Oh no, never! But giving new dishes a fair chance will certainly add some excitement to supper hour, and who knows where it might lead!

I think having a mix of structured activities and free time is key to creating variety in my leisure time. For instance, I have a regular book club that meets once a month where we discuss different books, which gives me something to look forward to every four weeks. On other evenings, I might spend some time tending to my garden, planting new vegetables or pruning flowers – there's always something changing seasonally that keeps me interested. And of course, I love spending time with my grandkids; whether it's playing games, watching films together, or going out for outings, those moments are always special. If I'm feeling more energetic, I'll take a walk around the local park or visit the nearby nature reserve, which helps me unwind. It all adds up to a nice balance of activities that cater to my moods and preferences.

I like keeping a little notebook where I jot down my plans and progress, especially with hobbies like gardening and cooking for the grandkids. It helps me remember what I need to buy, how much water to give them each week... all those sorts of thing. Sometimes I'll even draw pictures of our plants or meals we make together – it's lovely looking back on them afterwards! My granddaughter has started helping me with writing out reminders now, so it's nice having her input too. Nothing fancy, just something simple and easy to glance over whenever I need to check.

I'm quite particular about keeping my home tidy and organized. You know, a bit like how I used to be very meticulous during my secretarial work! Now, since retiring, I make sure to allocate some time each morning before breakfast to tackle any pending chores or paperwork. It helps me feel more focused throughout the day. For my calendar, I stick to using an analog planner – there's something soothing about putting pen to paper, isn't there? And I colour-code everything according to categories, which makes it easier for me to find what I need quickly. My family says it looks rather neat, too! In terms of digital organisation, I'm not super tech-savvy, so I tend to rely on simple tools like email reminders and notes on my phone. I've found that breaking down large tasks into smaller ones really helps; I'll jot them down and tick them off one by one. Plus, having a designated spot for all sorts of documents and papers keeps them out of sight and mind. It's funny, but sometimes I think being methodical has become second nature now. For instance, whenever we have visitors coming over, I always ask myself, "What do I need to prepare?" whether it's tidying the living room or making a list

Before I head out, I like to have a nice cup of tea, maybe some toast with butter or jam, and catch up on the news on my BBC radio while I'm getting ready. It gives me something to listen to while I tidy myself up, check the weather forecast, and make sure I haven't forgotten anything essential, such as taking my handbag or wallet. A bit of gentle stretching afterwards helps too – gets me feeling more alert and prepared for whatever the day brings. Then, I'll usually take a quick glance at what the grandkids need reminding them about, so we're all set for our daily routines. Off I go!

I try my best to be mindful of how much electricity we're using here, although to be honest, I'm a bit of an old-fashioned sort who likes the familiarity of our current setup. We have some draught-proofing measures like those draught excluder thingies at the bottom of doors, and I make sure to switch lights off when not needed. But if I had to think outside the box, I suppose investing in solar panels could be something worth considering. My son has mentioned them, though he says it might take a while to pay off, which is always been a concern for me. Still, every little helps, and I'd rather reduce waste whenever possible.

I'm afraid I must admit that I find all these new-fangled gadgets a bit bewildering! My son tries to set them up for me, but he knows better than to expect me to figure out how they work myself. To be honest, I'm still using my trusty old microwave and kettle – they're tried and true! That being said, I do appreciate having some assistance with simple tasks like controlling the lights or thermostat remotely. And I suppose having my tablets and smartphones helps keep me connected with loved ones who live far away. But overall, I think I'll stick to what I know and love: a warm cuppa made properly, baked goods fresh from the oven... those sorts of things bring more joy to my daily life than any smart device could!

I think I've found that prioritising is quite simple really. You know, my mum always used to say "if you make a list, tick them one by one." And I think that's stuck with me all these years. When faced with multiple demands on my time and resources, I try to ask myself what's most important in each situation. What will have the greatest impact, what will bring the most joy? For instance, I'd much rather spend an hour tending to my garden – watching those vegetables grow and knowing I'm providing something nourishing for my family. But if there was a pressing issue that needed attending to, like fixing the boiler, I wouldn't hesitate to tackle that first. It might be more mundane, but getting people warm and safe comes before enjoying the fruits of my labour. And I suppose that's where being a bit of a planner helps too. Writing everything out makes sure I remember what I need to do and in what order. Of course, flexibility is key; some plans inevitably go awry, but having a framework does give me peace of mind. Now, let's hope that son of mine learns his lesson about keeping our heating sorted...!
